titleStmt.title: Letter from J.M. Wilson to Edward Carson, 3 November 1915
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: J.M. Wilson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from J.M. Wilson to Edward Carson (1854-1935). Carson was a prominent Unionist Politician. Originally a practicing solicitor from Dublin, Carson's opposition to the proposed third Home Rule bill made him the figure head of Unionism throughout the island. He was the centerpiece of the speaking tour that culminated in Ulster Day (28 September 1912), when just under half a million signed Ulster's solemn league and covenant pledging to use ‘all means which may be found necessary to defeat the present conspiracy to set up a home rule parliament in Dublin’. Carson inaugurated the Larne gunrunning in 1914 which armed the UVF, many of whom Carson would encourage to join the British Army throughout the First World War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
RAILWAY STATION, M.G.W.R.
EDGEWORTHSTOWN.
TELEGRAMS —
BALLINALEE, 1 1/4 MILES. Private How 'cute of Redmond too, last night, in his fulsome
praise of W.A!
whom he once
refused to trust !! J.M. WILSON,
CURRYGRANE,
EDGEWOTHSTOWN. 3 NOV: 1915 Dear Sir Edward Every honest man will
be delighted with your
speech of last night, you
have done much to
expose the shocking os—
cillation wh: has gone
on. No need for me
to dilate on this — I know
only too much, expecially
from the military point
of view — But this
letter is just to say one
word regarding the Irish stand—
point of the war. Surely you, or, someone who
will be listened to will
expose the pestilent nonsense
talked by J.Redmond last
night both as regards his
Volunteers who melted when war was declared! (when, especially he
was talking 'through his hat' !)
and the bunkum about his
grievance over the Irish Regts:
not being appreciated — Poor
chaps they have fought
splendidly, and have earned
great praise from many
quarters as they fully deserve,
& all this talk of his is only a blind to de—
ceive England, & give him
some sort of an apparent
grievance which he will
try to exploit against
NatService being applied
to Ireland. One word of warning as
to above. If my information is good
it goes to show the ex—
—istence of a gigantic
conspiracy over here, headed
by Redmond, Devlin, — Dillon
and abetted by certain
high officials in the Castle,
which would result in
a differentiation being made
as regards Ireland if &
when Nat Service comes
on. You saw Devlins
speech at Kilkenny, on
Sunday last, I enclose you
J.Dillon's latest. What
right have these men to
indulge in such impertinent
assertions as to policy? It would be absolutely fatal
if Ireland were left out
of the scheme — Redmond only
represents farmers & shop boys,
noneof them are enlisting. Pray do not trouble to answer
this, you can have no
time. J Mackay Wilson
